critique.summary: The article outlines a limited-time promotion for Claude users that doubles usage limits during off-peak hours from March 13 to March 27, 2026. This promotion is applicable to Free, Pro, Max, and Team plans, but not to Enterprise plans.
mainPoints: Promotion doubles usage limits for Claude users outside 8 AM-2 PM ET/5-11 AM PT.
Promotion is valid from March 13, 2026 to March 27, 2026.
No action is required from users to participate in the promotion.
Bonus usage does not count against weekly usage limits.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article appear to be accurate based on the details provided about the promotion, including eligibility, duration, and conditions. There are no apparent discrepancies or misleading information.
considerations: The article does not mention how the promotion might affect user experience or server load during off-peak hours.
Potential implications for users who may not be aware of the promotion's time restrictions.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the implications of Claude's March 2026 usage promotion, with users sharing thoughts on pricing models, time zone issues, and psychological aspects of promotions. Some comments express skepticism while others suggest practical improvements.

comments.author: colingauvin
comments.text:
Presumably they have unused compute in those hours and figure they may as well enable people to use it and get more invested into their ecosystem.

What I wish Anthropic would do is be a lot more explicit about what windows apply when. Surely they have the data to say "you get X usage from hours A to B, Y usage from B to C"
comments.analysis: This comment provides a logical analysis of the promotion's intent and suggests a constructive improvement for clarity.

comments.author: JoshGlazebrook
comments.text: I just know there has to be some psychology in play with these promos. The promo during December got me to upgrade to the $100 plan, and I know I'm not the only one.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully connects promotional strategies to user behavior, suggesting a broader psychological impact.

comments.author: podviaznikov
comments.text: Travelling salesman problem in 2026 is Travelling Engineer Problem to find optimal location to maximize tokens usage.
comments.analysis: This comment presents a clever analogy but lacks context for broader understanding, making it less accessible.

comments.author: twtw99
comments.text: This is great, but i guess they are feeling the heat from Codex resetting limits in the last month quite a bit.
comments.analysis: The comment suggests a plausible reason for the promotion, but it lacks depth and evidence.

comments.author: daemonologist
comments.text: Would be cool to have a $5-10/month plan that only works off-peak, for people who want to do the occasional side project after work.  Right now it's hard to justify anything but Copilot (because it's cheaper, offers the same models, and I'm nowhere near the usage limits).
comments.analysis: While the suggestion is practical, it lacks a deeper analysis of the promotion's implications.

comments.author: rokhayakebe
comments.text: This company is clearly on a mission. I would just like to know what that mission is. I mean this in a good way.
comments.analysis: The comment expresses curiosity about the company's goals but does not contribute significantly to the discussion.

comments.author: walthamstow
comments.text: Dear line manager, I will be taking a very long lunch 12-6pm in London's Chinatown then heading back to the office half cut to vibe code
comments.analysis: This comment is humorous but detracts from the serious nature of the discussion.

comments.author: gslin
comments.text: Using timezone not UTC for a global service is a crime, especially mixed with daylight saving.
comments.analysis: The comment is overly aggressive and does not provide constructive feedback on the promotion.

comments.author: estebarb
comments.text: I didn't understood "your five-hour usage" I thought plans were per interaction or per token, not per hour.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects confusion but does not add value to the discussion or offer solutions.

comments.author: phendrenad2
comments.text: [delayed]
comments.analysis: This comment provides no content or insight, rendering it irrelevant to the discussion.

comments.author: andkenneth
comments.text: This is a psyop to recruit more Australians I'm sure of it
comments.analysis: The comment is conspiratorial and lacks credible reasoning, detracting from meaningful discourse.

comments.author: johnhamlin
comments.text: AI psychosis intensifies
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive and does not engage with the topic at hand.

comments.author: Analemma_
comments.text: Long ago in the ancient days of punchcards and IBM mainframes, you’d write your programs during the day, then submit them to run overnight and pick up your results in the morning. It would be funny and sort of romantic if time-based LLM pricing returned us to that: write your specs all day, run agents on them overnight, check out the results in the morning.
comments.analysis: While nostalgic and creative, this comment strays from the practical discussion of the promotion.

comments.author: michaelhoney
comments.text: Living in Tasmania as competitive advantage
comments.analysis: This comment is vague and lacks context, making it difficult to understand its relevance.

comments.author: qwertyuiop_
comments.text: I guess extra compute opened up after they were canned by Department of War.
comments.analysis: This comment is speculative and lacks factual basis, contributing little to the discussion.

comments.author: Footprint0521
comments.text: changes sleep schedule
comments.analysis: This comment is humorous but does not contribute meaningfully to the conversation.

comments.author: delduca
comments.text: Wtf is ET? Is an alien time?
comments.analysis: This comment is off-topic and does not engage with the main discussion.

comments.author: AussieWog93
comments.text: That is doubled usage between 5AM and 11PM for anyone playing along from Sydney/Melbourne.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a factual observation but lacks deeper analysis or context.

comments.author: egeozcan
comments.text: So afternoon in Germany or am I misreading?
comments.analysis: This comment reflects confusion but does not contribute to the discussion.

comments.author: timmg
comments.text:
I’m trying to figure out how this affects weekly limits, since those overlap peak hours. My observation is that it doesn’t. But I could be wrong.

If they are doing it “right” I think any off peak usage should count 50% toward your weekly limits.

Edit: it does look like they are doing it the "right" way.
comments.analysis: The comment raises an important question about usage limits but lacks definitive clarity.
